speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Rose Kaplovitz on the 3rd of April 1995. Tape three. Rose, you told me of a poem that you remembered about-- that dealt with the period you've been discussing. Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a Polish poem. I learned it in the ghetto in 1943. I have never thought about it until a year ago. I was with a friend in a car talking about the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, you know, there was a poem I learned in the ghetto. And he said, do you think you remember it? And I said, I don't know. And I started reciting. And it was like an instant recall.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that, I remember it until today. I know it in Polish. Maybe one day I'll translate it into English. But I don't see how the feeling could be translated. It's a beautiful poem. It's about-- really, it tells about the life in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who wrote it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I know somebody Jewish, of course, wrote it because there are a lot of people today that resent something that I would recite in Polish. But we have to remember, this was written by a Jew and was recited by the children of the ghetto. And you want me to say it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, if you can do it with the translation, it would be very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I'll tell the poem. And then I'll tell what it is about, OK? [SPEAKING POLISH] [SPEAKING POLISH] I don't know how that poem came back to me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: --after 50 years. That poem tells about spring coming to the ghetto, about the lilacs blooming, about reminding the spring that we are Jews, that before the war how we lived, and now we are locked up. But it also tells about the children whose skin is pale, whose eyes are sad, who play in the dirty yards where it smells of death, who are hungry. It tells about dreams that they dream at night about guillotines, about death, about the Nazis, about the dreams that they have and about the grave of their father, whose greenery has not covered yet because he was just thrown into a grave. It tells about the hunger. It tells about the loneliness. It tells about the horrible life of the child. And it goes on about this horrible thing, about the brother that was being called to report, and he didn't yet. And about-- but it finishes on a note-- the spring is coming. The lilac is in bloom. The lilac is in bloom. But God, if you are, then SOS-- save our souls. It finishes on that note of hope. And but it's very emotional if you understand the words. It's difficult for me to translate. Perhaps one day I'll sit down and maybe I'll do justice to it by translating. Such was life. And it describes the life in the ghetto in a beautiful but a sad way, of course. Anyway, when we go back to the ghetto, I was assigned to work in a factory outside of the ghetto. Mostly young children, such as myself, were working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was that time not quite 13. We were making dominoes and checkers for the German armies. So in a way, we were helping those whose name it was to wipe us out. We would march because you could only leave the ghetto or enter the ghetto if you were escorted by Nazi guards. So we would be marched to work in the morning and then marched back. But perhaps it was better because there was nothing for me to do in the ghetto all day long. Games in the ghetto-- there were none. Even hide and seek no longer was a game. It was a way of life. The Nazis would try to seek us out. We would like to hide. So there were no games left for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours did you work in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, between eight and 10 hours. But many times, we worked different hours. We would be going to work and we would see children going to school singing, singing songs that we knew. And here we were going to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was such a sad march, such a heartbreaking-- I cannot describe to you what a child feels when he marches in a line escorted by Nazi-- by guards, and you see across the street children like yourself going to school, carrying books, laughing, playing, singing. And you always wonder why, why, why? But there are no answers. Was it all girls in this factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, boys and girls, but all, all very young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they feed you during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, during the day we were not fed, only when we returned to the ghetto. That's when we got our rations of food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the rations?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, I don't recall exactly because it was per family or whatever. I remember the food that I was fed once I got to concentration camp. But I would like to get to that later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the Judenrate? What do you remember of, of their role in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrate. Shortly before the Judenrate-- of course, it was unknown to me at that time-- my father came home and told me, please, sit down. And he sat down with me and tried to tell me that he found out from my cousin who worked in the Judenrate, which is a Jewish community-- the heads of Jewish community-- that he found out from them that only families with one child would be allowed to remain in the ghetto. Everybody else, he told me, would be resettled to God knows where. And they are asking for volunteers for Ober Altstadt camp, labor camp. And since two of my sisters were there, although each one went at different times, somehow by the hand of God or whatever, they were united and ended up in the same camp. And he said, since they're asking for volunteers-- they never ask for volunteers. But again, they deceived us. We never knew what they were doing or why. He said-- he suggested, perhaps I volunteer. And at first, I thought my father, like so many fathers, like so many parents whose children were taken away from them have gone mad. And I thought, perhaps my father has gone mad, suggesting that I, his child, volunteer. I cried. I said, I don't want to go. I don't want to leave. But he tried to convince me. He said, this way, you see, your mother, your younger sister, and I would be allowed to remain here. And you would be with your two sisters. And besides, the war is coming to an end. It's just a matter of several months. We will be reunited pretty soon. It will be all over. Having really no choice and always believing my father, I decided that I have to do what my father wants me to do. And I cannot begin to describe to you my goodbyes to my mother the next morning. Of those seven children, there were two of us left. And I, too, was leaving. My mother's hair turned completely gray. She weighed under 80 pounds. I think that, by that time, my mother no longer cared to live. I think my mother gave up on life. She was holding onto me, couldn't let go. I didn't want to let go. But my father took my hand and said, come on, we have to go. And my father, holding my hand, led me to the Umschlagplatz, to the point where I had to report. On the way, he tried to instill hope in me and kept on saying, this is coming to an end soon. Remember, we shall be waiting for you. Remember, you have to come back. So be strong and live and come back because we wait for you. I didn't realize then that my father knew that the end is so near. And he fabricated this tale to perhaps give me a little more time to perhaps help me survive. I believed what my father told me was true. Had I known what happened only two weeks after I have left, I would never have made it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I try to think of my father-- having children-- what they did to this man. He, who was ready to give his life for his little girl, for me. He, having to take me by my hand, delivering to the Nazis. He, who was ready to die to save me, finding himself so totally, totally-- rendering him so totally useless, so powerless, how terrible he must have felt. What have they done to our people, even before they killed us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the Durchgangslager-- like that was a temporary camp from which I was to be shipped eventually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the locket, the locket.

speaker: interviewee
text: And while there, they asked us to give up any valuables. Like, I had a tiny little suitcase with me, in which I had my little album with photographs of my girlfriends. And I had a little book in which all my girlfriends wrote something for me, you know, from before the war. This was my personal possession. And I also owned a little silver chain. I had a little medallion which I was given for my birthday by my oldest sister Regina. It has a Moses. It's here. On one side, it's a Moses. It did not have that gold, gold frame around it, just a little medallion with Moses on one side and with the words [HEBREW] on the other side. And when I was forced to give up that little silver chain, I took it off, took the little medallion off. It is not a valuable medallion, but it's so precious to me. I gave the chain up. And I put the medallion in my mouth and kept it there. And it survived with me through the war. It's the most precious piece of jewelry--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for me today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Turn it around.

speaker: interviewee
text: Moses on one side with the 10 commandments and the shema, which is one of the holiest prayers of the Jewish people. And to me, this is the most precious piece of jewelry that I possess because of the value that it has of remembering my sister. And always, whenever I wear it, I think of her. My sister, by the way-- I'll tell you about my sister. When I left for concentration camp, not knowing that only two weeks after I left, our ghetto was declared Judenrein. And all the life of the Jewish people that existed for hundreds of years in my hometown came to an end. And all the people who lived there perished. Among them, of course, my older sister, Regina, who had a little girl. She was by that time either four or five years old, I don't know even. And from my brother-in-law who survived the war, I found out what happened. Because when she arrived in Auschwitz, she was strong. She was not like me, small. She was tall, strong. And she was picked for labor, which meant that she perhaps could have survived. But in order to go and live, she had to take her little girl, set her aside, and walk away from her. What a choice this must have been. She chose to take her little girl, picked her up, and as my brother-in-law told me, she would not part from her. And therefore, she went into the gas chamber with her little girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: For many years after the war, I could not forgive her for not living. For I felt that since her little girl, whom I adored, couldn't survive, why didn't she live? And I carried that thought in my mind until my very first child was born. And it was then that I knew that what my sister Regina did, I would have done too for my child. And that was then that I could forgive her for not surviving. Anyway, I arrived in Ober Altstadt concentration camp. Can you tell me about the journey there? How did they transport you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They transported us on special trains. Only our group was in-- it wasn't a big train because there were only a few of us going. So it was a regular train. But we were in a separate wagon, closed in and guarded. And unlike all the people that went in those cattle cars, we did not. And we arrived in Ober Altstadt, and it was a camp that was attached to the factory grounds. So when we were being marched to work, we did not have to go through the streets of the city, the small town. It was-- you know, there were long, gray barracks. We were assigned about 30 girls to a room. We slept on bunk beds. We were given a straw sack that served as a mattress, a pillow, a blanket. All our clothing was taken away from us. We were given a uniform with a number.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the day that I entered the concentration camp, all my earthly possessions were taken away from me, even the very last-- my name. And I just became a number among so many other numbers. I cannot describe to you the feeling what it is when even your name no longer is yours, when you are just a number among so many other numbers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our food consisted of a slice of dark bread, a cup of black coffee, a bowl of watery soup. And on that nourishment, we had to sustain ourselves while working 12-hour shifts. At first, my sisters went for the rations. And they asked that they give them one piece of bread for all three of us. They had a reason for doing that until I discovered what they were doing. They tried to give me a little bit more bread than they had. But when I found out, then I asked for separate rations because I didn't want them-- although I was very hungry, I knew that they were just as hungry. And I did no longer want them to share with me and give up of their food.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you make contact with your sisters who were already there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I arrived in the camp, my sisters were at work. As a matter of fact, one of my sisters heard from the girls that just came for the new shift that arrived in the factory-- she heard from them that a girl by the name of Zaks arrived in the camp. She says, oh, I bet you it's one of my cousins. You cannot imagine, you cannot picture, the sight of when she came from factory-- and my other sister-- and there I stood, waiting for them. I cannot describe to you the shock. They realized that something is wrong. Why am I, at my age-- well, I lied about my age and so did my father because by all rights, I should not have been in concentration camp. I should not have been sent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were too young?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too young.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you say here?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father said that I was 16. I didn't know, and I said 15. But somehow-- they also looked for girls with small hands. And that sort of saved me because when the hauptfuhrer asked, 'kannst du arbeiten?' I said, yeah-- can you work? Because at first, I was assigned to a very long, long machine. It had tiny little spindles, you know, like that. And the spool of thread was right in the middle. If that spool tore or got stuck, you had to stop the spindle one at a time, while the rest of the machine was running. So if you had big hands, you couldn't do it. So all the girls with small hands were assigned to that machine. What we were producing is, out of flax, we were making thread. And of course, eventually, the finished product was a German uniform. Again, we were working to help the Nazis fight the war against us. As I said, we worked 12- to 14-hour shifts. And every time, when my machine was giving me problems, I was kept busy. But it was the time when the machine was running smoothly that it was the worst because it was then that I had time to daydream, contemplate, think of home. And I always thought of home, of my parents, of the holidays, of Passover, of Friday night, the table bedecked with all kinds of goodies and the whole family sitting around and singing.

speaker: interviewee
text: How happy I was. How I missed those days. But how hungry I was. And all that food on the table-- and here I was so hungry, so very hungry. How does one describe a hunger? You know, I would have bitten into the machine if I could chew it. How do I tell you what-- how hungry I was? Hunger was a daily problem faced by all the inmates.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, you know, hunger is a devastating weapon. Hunger strips you first of your strength, of your dignity, of your pride. Hunger also strips you of your identity. Hunger also strips you sometimes of the will to fight back and often also of your will to live, to go on. And once you gave up, once you did not care, you perished. There was nothing to sustain you. But, you know, even though starvation depleted our bodies, the link among the prisoners who shared the same room provided a life-sustaining force. And a feeling of solidarity tended to develop among the girls who shared the same room. We would help each other. We would give courage to each other so as to maintain some kind of order and unity in our miserable life. There were no stealings and there was no squealing in our camp. You know, if a girl would break down in the middle of the night and it seemed like she no longer cares or can go on, the rest of the girls would come and sit with her and talk to her just to help her make through that one more night, always hoping that perhaps tomorrow things will look better, that perhaps tomorrow this will end. We always looked for the future, for tomorrow. Had we known-- had I known, in 1943, in August, that I would live in the concentration camp for two years, I think I would have given up a long time before. It was always that tomorrow, things will change. We didn't look far ahead into the future, only until tomorrow. You know, many times when a girl was so sad and everybody was crying, somebody would start singing. And often, it was my sister Tola, who sang so beautiful, would start singing. Because song has a means of expressing sorrow, pain, but also hope, courage. And it was often that when our spirit sank, when we felt at the lowest point in our life, that suddenly somebody would start. And the rest of us often would join in a song.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, our state of our mental life was reduced to the lowest possible level. I haven't seen a written material for so many years. I haven't been so cut off from the rest of the world physically as well as mentally. What did it do to a human brain?

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow, or maybe in spite of everything, we managed to preserve the vestiges of our spiritual freedom of mind. You know, religion was a very important part of our lives. And we tried somehow in concentration camp to remember holidays or Shabbat, not that we had a calendar to go by--

speaker: interviewer
text: Weren't you punished if you tried to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Observe?

speaker: interviewer
text: --observe a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no way of observing. But you know what? On Yom Kippur, which is a day of fast by the Jewish people, this was the time that, if ever, we were given better food or a little bit more or a piece of meat, it would be on Yom Kippur. And they would keep that in mind that it is that day that we are not supposed to eat. And they would try to give us food to discourage us from fasting. Although many girls did fast, I did not. I was not that courageous. And I was too hungry to think of the holiday. But somehow, many of the girls did observe the holidays or try to remember or try to say a prayer, though it was almost impossible. Many times we would come from work hungry, cold, tired. We would be called to [GERMAN], which meant roll call, where we would be forced to stand outdoors in all kinds of weather. And while standing there, our Nazi guards would think about ways of humiliating us, degrading us. They would squeeze out of us the last reserves of human dignity. These guards were males as well as females. They were human beings. After the war, many of them during the Nazi trials tried to claim that they followed orders. But they did not because Hitler was not in our concentration camp. Hitler was not there telling them what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: How does a human being become a beast? What makes him do such things to others? I believe that they only could do that to us by making themselves believe-- or by their ideology that made them believe that indeed, they are superior human beings. And we, in turn, are subhuman, or an 'Untermensch,' as they called us. I believe that only then they could carry out those horrendous crimes.
